Business Effect: Leadership cannot see concentration risk, privileged paths, or data exposure
NIST SP 800 53 Control Family: Program Management, Supply Chain Risk Management
Evidence: Authoritative vendor inventory, owner attestations, data flow diagrams
Target State: All vendors tiered, owned, reviewed, and mapped to data access

Business Effect: Dormant permissions allow attackers to move through old trust relationships
Evidence: Quarterly access review, role matrix, revoked token logs
Target State: No standing privileged vendor access without active business justification

Business Effect: Control design exists on paper but lacks current proof
NIST SP 800 53 Control Family: Assessment Authorization and Monitoring, Supply Chain Risk Management
Evidence: Current SOC 2 report, questionnaire validation, exception approval
Target State: Renewals blocked when evidence is expired or incomplete

Business Effect: Review quality drops and weak evidence passes because the process rewards completion
Evidence: Reviewer sampling, quality score, training simulation results
Target State: Reviewers validate evidence quality, not just checkbox completion

Business Effect: Detection lag increases because alerts lack unified context
Evidence: Log source inventory, SIEM use cases, alert tuning evidence
Target State: Tier 1 vendor activity centralized with tested alert rules

Business Effect: Disclosure and containment slow down during legal and communication conflict
Evidence: Tabletop exercise, escalation matrix, communication approvals
Target State: Incident authority and disclosure paths are documented and tested
